skill.name: ffind
skill.description: Finds and identifies file types in firmware images, then optionally extracts ext2, ext3, ext4, or F2FS filesystems for security analysis.
skill.summary: Analyze firmware files, identify embedded file types, and extract supported filesystems with ffind.
skill.author: BrownFineSecurity

security_audit.summary: The static external command and sudo findings are true positives because the skill instructs agents to run ffind on user-supplied paths and use sudo for extraction. The temp directory findings are also real, while the weak cryptography findings are false positives from filesystem version text, not cryptographic code. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, so the skill is not blocked but should not publish without human review and stronger safety guidance.

high_findings.title: Sudo-Based Filesystem Extraction
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill states that extraction requires sudo and shows sudo ffind commands. Running filesystem extraction as root on untrusted firmware can expose the host to parser, mount, and file ownership risks.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly requires root privileges and provides sudo examples. The purpose is legitimate firmware analysis, but the privilege boundary risk is direct and material.

medium_findings.title: External Command Execution on User Paths
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs agents to run ffind commands against paths supplied by the user. This is expected for the tool, but it can still execute local tooling against untrusted files and directories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documented examples, not hidden behavior, and they operate on user-selected paths. No shell metacharacter handling guidance is present.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Writes to Temporary Extraction Paths
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill documents default extraction under /tmp and a custom /tmp extraction example. Extraction can create many files, preserve unusual metadata, or overwrite careless output locations if not isolated.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The extraction paths are explicitly documented. The risk is contextual because temporary directories are normal for analysis, but untrusted filesystem contents require isolation.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Finding Is Not Supported
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The static weak cryptography hits appear tied to ext2, ext3, ext4, or F2FS filesystem text. No cryptographic algorithm, hash function, or encryption operation is described in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines describe filesystem formats, not cryptographic primitives. I found no evidence of weak crypto implementation or recommendation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged External Tool Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill combines external command execution with sudo-based extraction. This pattern should require explicit user confirmation, sandboxing, and trusted output directories before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both example extraction flows invoke ffind with sudo. The pattern is intentional and useful, but it raises host safety risk.

content.value_statement: Firmware reviews often need quick file identification before deeper reverse engineering starts. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through ffind workflows for file type discovery and supported filesystem extraction.
actual_capabilities: Guides analysis of one or more files or directories with ffind.
Identifies file types in firmware and IoT device artifacts.
Supports artifact-focused output or all detected file types.
Explains text, JSON, and quiet output modes for ffind.
Documents extraction of ext2, ext3, ext4, and F2FS filesystems.
Allows custom extraction directories when using ffind extraction mode.
limitations: Requires the ffind command to be installed and available to the agent environment.
Filesystem extraction requires external tools such as e2fsprogs, f2fs-tools, and util-linux.
Extraction requires sudo privileges, which raises host safety risk.
The skill does not provide malware containment, sandbox setup, or firmware provenance checks.

use_cases.title: Triage Firmware Images
use_cases.description: Identify embedded file types and prioritize artifacts for deeper inspection before reverse engineering.

use_cases.title: Inspect IoT Device Dumps
use_cases.description: Locate supported filesystems and extract their contents for configuration, binary, and credential review.

use_cases.title: Prepare Evidence Summaries
use_cases.description: Use readable output modes to summarize discovered file types for tickets, reports, or handoff notes.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze One Firmware File
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ffind to analyze this firmware file for artifact file types: [path]. Summarize the main findings and do not extract filesystems.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Multiple Inputs
prompt_templates.prompt: Run ffind against these paths: [paths]. Show all detected file types and explain which files deserve deeper review.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Safe Extraction
prompt_templates.prompt: Assess whether ffind extraction is needed for [path]. If it is needed, propose a safe output directory and list the sudo command for my approval.

prompt_templates.title: Generate Report Findings
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze [path] with ffind, include relevant output mode choices, and turn the results into a concise firmware analysis summary.

output_examples.input: Analyze a firmware image without extraction.
output_examples.output: The result would summarize detected artifact categories, highlight uncommon file types, and recommend whether extraction is worth the added risk.

output_examples.input: Prepare an extraction workflow for a suspected ext4 image.
output_examples.output: The response would identify required tools, request approval before sudo use, and specify an isolated extraction directory.

output_examples.input: Review two firmware files and show all file types.
output_examples.output: The response would compare the file type distribution across both inputs and call out artifacts relevant to security analysis.

best_practices: Run ffind on copies of firmware files, not original evidence.
Use an isolated working directory with enough disk space for extraction.
Require explicit user approval before any sudo-based extraction.
anti_patterns: Do not run sudo extraction on untrusted firmware without isolation.
Do not extract into shared or sensitive directories.
Do not assume file type detection proves a file is safe.

faq.question: What does this skill help analyze?
faq.answer: It helps analyze firmware files, directories, and embedded filesystems using the ffind command.

faq.question: Does it extract filesystems automatically?
faq.answer: No. The skill describes extraction options, but extraction should require explicit user approval.

faq.question: Which filesystems are mentioned?
faq.answer: The skill mentions ext2, ext3, ext4, and F2FS filesystem extraction.

faq.question: Why is sudo a risk?
faq.answer: Sudo gives extraction tools elevated host privileges while processing untrusted firmware data.

faq.question: Can it output machine-readable results?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents a JSON output mode, along with text and quiet modes.

faq.question: Is this safe for direct marketplace publication?
faq.answer: No. It needs human review because it instructs privileged filesystem extraction and external command execution.
